Jae Woong Hwang Resume Experience Toolchains

Phase 1: Foundation

Turret Motion Stabilization on Mobile Platforms

Master's thesis at KAIST focused on building a real embedded motion-control system for a turret mounted on a moving platform. This page distills the work into a systems-architecture narrative: physical bottleneck, core control math, and quantitative proof on actual hardware.

Back to resume
Foundation

Control, Estimation, and Real-Hardware Validation

This project built the mathematical and hardware foundation for later work in vehicle controls, EKF-based estimation, and production validation. The key point is not just that it was a thesis, but that the control logic was closed-loop tested on real hardware under physical disturbance.

Problem: The Physical Bottleneck

  • Coupled disturbances: performance degraded under unpredictable base-motion dynamics and nonlinear friction in the physical hardware.
  • Sensor noise: high-frequency INS/Gyro and MTF sensor noise made simple PID or rule-based control insufficient for precision tracking.
  • Engineering meaning: the problem required a controller that understood plant behavior, not just a software-side quick fix.

Architecture & Math: The Core Logic

Two-Mass Dynamics Modeling

Modeled the physical plant as a two-mass spring-damper system, then used loop-shaping to identify and isolate resonance behavior before controller tuning.

Time Delay Estimation (TDE)

Avoided heavy full-order modeling of unknown nonlinear dynamics by estimating and canceling the continuous disturbance term in real time.

dtot(tk) ≈ dtot(tk-1) = τm(tk-1) − J̄ · ω̇(tk-1)

Sensor Fusion via Kalman Filter

Designed a discrete-time Kalman Filter to fuse INS/Gyro and MTF data, filtering high-frequency noise and improving pod velocity estimation.

x̂k|k−1 = A x̂k−1|k−1 + B uk−1

Result: Quantitative Proof

  • Successfully deployed the C/C++ control logic onto actual physical hardware.
  • Significantly reduced baseline position error and achieved stable tracking close to zero steady-state error under artificial base-motion disturbance.
  • Demonstrated that the proposed logic was not only mathematically valid, but physically verified on a real system.

The two figures below are retained from the original page: one shows measured tracking performance, and the other proves that the controller was applied to actual turret hardware for verification.

Verification Evidence
